original_hypothesis.title: checkSufficientLiquidity Reports False Positive Under Cross-Pool Balance Competition
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: checkSufficientLiquidity validates against the pool's internal reserveValidatorToken accounting, not the contract's actual ERC-20 balance. When multiple pools share the same validatorToken, the aggregate reserves can equal the actual balance. If any pool's accounting becomes desynchronized (via fee-on-transfer tokens, unchecked transfer return values, or reentrancy in another pool), checkSufficientLiquidity reports sufficient liquidity while the actual balance is insufficient. External protocols relying on this check to pre-validate swaps will encounter unexpected reverts during executeFeeSwap.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: checkSufficientLiquidity is the only pre-flight check function. External integrators (payment routers, multi-hop swap aggregators) likely call it before committing to an executeFeeSwap path. A false positive causes cascading failures in multi-step transactions that have already committed other resources.
deployment_assumptions: Multiple pools share the same validatorToken
At least one pool has experienced accounting desync (e.g., fee-on-transfer token in another pool)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No token validation in mint(). checkSufficientLiquidity at FeeAMM.sol:89-96 checks only pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken.

proof_of_signal.pattern: checkSufficientLiquidity checks pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken (internal accounting) without comparing to IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)); executeFeeSwap trusts same internal accounting

preconditions: Multiple pools use the same validatorToken
At least one pool's internal accounting exceeds actual token balance (from fee-on-transfer, unchecked return, or reentrancy)
External protocol relies on checkSufficientLiquidity as a pre-validation gate

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: checkSufficientLiquidity: amountOutNeeded = (maxAmount * M) / SCALE; check is against internal reserves only
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Desynchronize one pool's accounting (e.g., via fee-on-transfer token), then checkSufficientLiquidity passes for a different pool but actual transfer fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Indirect: causes DoS/failure for integrating protocols, potential griefing of multi-step transactions

economic_questions: Are there external protocols that rely on checkSufficientLiquidity to route swaps?
What is the cost to an integrating protocol when a pre-validated swap reverts?
How easily can accounting desync be triggered in a neighboring pool?
validation_requirements: Create two pools sharing validatorToken, desync one via fee-on-transfer, verify checkSufficientLiquidity still passes for the other
Confirm executeFeeSwap reverts with actual balance shortfall despite checkSufficientLiquidity passing

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal: just need to trigger accounting desync in one pool
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: griefing impact on integrating protocols

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After accounting desync in any pool sharing the token

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Failed transactions, wasted gas, potential fund locking in multi-step operations
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on integrating protocol's transaction value

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare internal reserves vs actual balances under various desync scenarios

validation_result.impact_summary: checkSufficientLiquidity checks only per-pool internal reserves (not actual ERC-20 balance), which can be desynchronized via fee-on-transfer tokens or unchecked transfer return values, causing the function to report sufficient liquidity when the contract lacks actual tokens to fulfill the swap.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that checkSufficientLiquidity (FeeAMM.sol:89-96) only validates against pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken without ever consulting IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)). This internal accounting can diverge from the contract's actual token balance through multiple documented code defects: (1) No SafeERC20 usage means transfers returning false don't revert, allowing state updates without actual token movement; (2) Fee-on-transfer tokens cause the contract to credit full amounts to reserves while receiving fewer tokens; (3) Multiple pools can share the same validatorToken with no restriction, meaning reserve inflation in one pool depletes the actual balance available for other pools.

The cross-pool scenario works as follows: Pool A (tokenX, tokenZ) has reserveValidatorToken=500 (accurate). Pool B (tokenY, tokenZ) has reserveValidatorToken=1000 (inflated by 100 due to fee-on-transfer). Actual tokenZ balance is 1400. If Pool B executes a swap withdrawing 950, Pool B's reserve becomes 50, actual balance becomes 450. Now checkSufficientLiquidity for Pool A with amountOut=480 passes (500 >= 480), but executeFeeSwap will revert because only 450 tokens are available.

Even in a single-pool scenario, if a pool uses a fee-on-transfer token as validatorToken, the internal reserve will exceed actual balance, and checkSufficientLiquidity will give false positives.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 89-96 confirm checkSufficientLiquidity only checks internal accounting. Lines 98-119 confirm executeFeeSwap relies on the same internal accounting plus actual ERC-20 transfer. All transfer/transferFrom calls throughout the contract lack return value checks (no SafeERC20). The mint function (lines 163-209) credits full amountValidatorToken to reserves without measuring actual balance received. No restriction prevents multiple pools from sharing the same validatorToken.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Checks only pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken (internal accounting) without verifying IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)). When internal accounting exceeds actual balance (via fee-on-transfer, unchecked returns, or cross-pool balance competition), this function incorrectly reports sufficient liquidity.

Credits full amountValidatorToken to reserves without checking return value of transferFrom and without measuring actual balance change. Fee-on-transfer tokens cause reserve inflation. Non-reverting tokens returning false cause phantom credits.

The outgoing transfer on line 116 can revert if the contract's actual validatorToken balance is less than amountOut, even though the internal reserve check on line 108 passed. This creates the false positive that checkSufficientLiquidity predicted the swap would succeed.

calling_sequence: 1. External protocol calls checkSufficientLiquidity(userToken, validatorToken, maxAmount) to pre-validate swap
2. Function computes poolId = keccak256(abi.encode(userToken, validatorToken)) at line 94
3. Function computes amountOutNeeded = (maxAmount * M) / SCALE at line 95
4. Function checks pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken >= amountOutNeeded at line 96 — passes based on inflated internal accounting
5. External protocol then calls executeFeeSwap(userToken, validatorToken, amountIn)
6. executeFeeSwap passes internal reserve check at line 108
7. IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(msg.sender, amountOut) at line 116 reverts because actual balance < amountOut
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability manifests when internal reserves (pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken) exceed the contract's actual ERC-20 balance. This desync is caused by: (a) fee-on-transfer tokens where mint() at line 200 credits full amountValidatorToken but fewer tokens arrive; (b) unchecked transfer returns where transferFrom returns false but execution continues; (c) cross-pool competition where multiple pools claim the same token's balance. checkSufficientLiquidity at line 96 only checks per-pool internal accounting, not actual balance, so it reports sufficient liquidity. executeFeeSwap at line 116 then fails when the actual ERC-20 transfer cannot be fulfilled.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Multiple pools share the same validatorToken
At least one pool has experienced accounting desync (e.g., fee-on-transfer token)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided beyond foundry.toml which only specifies compiler settings. No token whitelists, pool configuration, or token restrictions are specified. The contract itself imposes no restrictions on which tokens can be used or how many pools can share the same validatorToken.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: Two pools share validatorToken (USDT-like). Pool B is created with a fee-on-transfer wrapper that takes 2% on transfers. When 10000 tokens are minted into Pool B, only 9800 arrive but reserveValidatorToken records 10000 (FeeAMM.sol:200). Pool A has correctly synchronized reserves of 5000. Total claimed reserves: 15000, actual balance: 14800. After Pool B swaps out 9700 (reserveValidatorToken becomes 300, actual balance becomes 5100), Pool A's checkSufficientLiquidity for 5050 passes (5000 < 5050... wait this doesn't work). Let me correct: if amountOutNeeded = 5000, check passes (5000 >= 5000), but if another operation has already consumed some balance, the transfer may fail. The more precise scenario: after Pool B operations drain extra tokens beyond Pool B's fair share due to inflated accounting, Pool A's fair share of actual tokens is less than Pool A's reserveValidatorToken.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium impact. The defect causes checkSufficientLiquidity to be unreliable as a pre-flight validation check. External integrators that use this function to route transactions or make commitments in multi-step operations will experience unexpected reverts. This leads to wasted gas, failed multi-step transactions, and potential loss of opportunity costs. The severity depends on how many integrators rely on this function and whether fee-on-transfer or non-standard tokens are used in pools.
recommended_mitigations: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 for all transfer/transferFrom calls to ensure failed transfers revert
Add actual balance verification in checkSufficientLiquidity: require(IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)) >= amountOutNeeded)
Measure actual balance changes (before/after pattern) instead of trusting nominal amounts, to handle fee-on-transfer tokens
Consider maintaining a global reserve tracker per token to ensure the sum of all pool reserves for a token doesn't exceed actual balance
Add token validation/whitelisting to prevent incompatible tokens from being used in pools
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FeeAMM. 2. Deploy a standard ERC20 (tokenZ) and a fee-on-transfer ERC20 wrapper (tokenZ_fot, 5% fee). 3. Deploy two user tokens (tokenX, tokenY). 4. Create Pool A: mint(tokenX, tokenZ, 10000, deployer). 5. Create Pool B: mint(tokenY, tokenZ_fot, 10000, deployer) — Pool B records 10000 but only 9500 arrive. 6. From Pool B, executeFeeSwap to drain most of Pool B's claimed reserves. 7. Verify checkSufficientLiquidity(tokenX, tokenZ, amount) still passes for Pool A. 8. Call executeFeeSwap on Pool A and observe revert due to insufficient actual balance.
uncertainties: Whether fee-on-transfer tokens are actually deployed as validatorTokens in production — no deployment scripts specify token constraints
Whether external protocols actually use checkSufficientLiquidity as a routing decision function
The exact token implementations that will be used — standard ERC20s with correct return values would mitigate some (but not all) desync vectors
Whether the unchecked-return-value path is exploitable depends on the specific ERC20 implementation (many modern tokens revert rather than return false)
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with fee-on-transfer tokens to quantify reserve divergence over time
Test with tokens that return false on failed transfers to verify accounting desync
Integration test: verify checkSufficientLiquidity output matches executeFeeSwap behavior under various desync conditions
Test cross-pool scenarios with shared validatorToken to measure balance competition effects
